disease.name: Acute Liver Failure and Hepatic Encephalopathy

emergencyPriority.notes: Acute liver failure rapidly progresses to coma and death without urgent recognition, ICP management, and liver transplant evaluation. Cerebral edema is the leading cause of death in ALF.

mustNotMiss: Cerebral edema with intracranial hypertension (leading cause of death in ALF)
King's College Criteria for urgent transplant listing in acetaminophen and non-acetaminophen ALF
Covert hepatic encephalopathy: subtle neuropsychiatric impairment with prolonged reaction time, reversal on treatment
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis as precipitant of hepatic encephalopathy in cirrhosis
Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) with extrahepatic organ failures
Wilsonian acute liver failure: Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, low alkaline phosphatase, high bilirubin, Kayser-Fleischer rings
diagnosticClues: West Haven criteria: Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (tri, sleep inversion, personality change), Grade 2 (lethargy, asterixis, confusion), Grade 3 (somnolent, incomprehensible speech), Grade 4 (coma)
Asterixis: bilateral asynchronous flap of outstretched hands (sensitive but not specific for hepatic encephalopathy)
Elevated ammonia > 150-200 ug/dL correlates with cerebral edema risk in ALF
Acute liver failure defined as: INR > 1.5, any degree of encephalopathy, onset < 26 weeks without cirrhosis
Multifocal slow waves on EEG (triphasic waves in grade 3-4 encephalopathy)
exclusionClues: Normal ammonia levels (< 50 ug/dL) make hepatic encephalopathy unlikely (though can be normal in chronic HE)
Rapidly improving transaminases without improving INR suggests irreversible liver failure (critical distinction)
Negative viral serologies, toxicology screen, and autoimmune markers narrow differential for ALF etiology
Resolution of encephalopathy with lactulose + rifaximin supports diagnosis of HE
Normal head CT and lumbar puncture exclude structural CNS causes of altered mental status

tools.name: King's College Criteria for Acetaminophen ALF
classification: OR: INR > 6.5 + Cr > 3.4 mg/dL + grade 3-4 encephalopathy
OR: Lactate > 3 mmol/L after fluid resuscitation
Any criteria met: high mortality without transplant

tools.name: King's College Criteria for Non-Acetaminophen ALF
classification: INR > 6.5 AND any encephalopathy irrespective of grade
OR: Any 3 of: age < 10 or > 40, unfavorable etiology, encephalopathy > 7 days before jaundice, INR > 3.5, bilirubin > 17.5 mg/dL
Unfavorable etiologies: seronegative hepatitis, drug-induced, Wilson disease, Budd-Chiari

tools.name: MELD-Na Score
tools.interpretation: Used for transplant allocation in chronic liver disease. Score > 40 predicts 90% 3-month mortality.

tools.name: Model for End-Stage Liver Disease (MELD) 3.0
tools.interpretation: Updated 2021 to include albumin and sex; better predictive accuracy for mortality on waitlist.

historyFeatures: Onset timing: acute (minutes/hours, ALF) vs subacute/chronic (days/weeks, cirrhosis decompensation)
Jaundice duration: encephalopathy onset before jaundice suggests subacute presentation
Past or current jaundice, GI bleeding, ascites, SBP, hepatic encephalopathy

Etiology: acetaminophen overdose (most common in US/UK), viral (hepatitis A/B/E, EBV, HSV in immunocompromised), drug-induced (isoniazid, valproate, halothane, propylthiouracil, amiodarone), mushroom poisoning (Amanita phalloides), autoimmune hepatitis, Wilson disease, Budd-Chiari syndrome, ischemic hepatitis (shock liver)
Alcohol use pattern, IV drug use, transfusion history, travel, sexual history
Family history of liver disease, Wilson disease, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
examFeatures: Neurologic: asterixis (grade 1-2), hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski sign (early), decerebrate/decorticate posturing (late, grade 4), sluggish pupillary response (early sign of herniation)
Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly (acute hepatitis, ALF), normal/small liver (fulminant), ascites, caput medusae, splenomegaly (portal hypertension)
Skin: jaundice, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, Terry nails, Dupuytren contractures (chronic disease)
HEENT: Kayser-Fleischer rings (Wilson disease), fetor hepaticus (musty sweet breath from mercaptans)
Parotid enlargement, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy (chronic liver disease)
riskFactors: Pre-existing cirrhosis (most common predisposition for HE; 30-50% develop HE)

Acute decompensation triggers: infection (SBP, UTI, pneumonia), GI bleeding, electrolyte imbalance (hyponatremia, hypokalemia), constipation, high-protein meal, TIPS procedure, sedatives/opioids, portosystemic shunt, surgery
Acetaminophen: > 4 g/day in adults; higher risk with fasting, alcohol use, CYP3A4 induction
Viral hepatitis: HAV in older adults, HEV in pregnant women (third trimester, high mortality)
Immunosuppression: HSV hepatitis in transplant/BMT patients, EBV in X-linked lymphoproliferative disease

subtypes.name: Acute Liver Failure (ALF) with Encephalopathy
subtypes.pathophysiology: Massive hepatocyte necrosis, loss of metabolic and synthetic function, accumulation of neurotoxins (ammonia, glutamine, inflammatory cytokines), leading to cerebral edema and intracranial hypertension

subtypes.name: Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure (ACLF)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Cirrhosis with acute decompensation and extrahepatic organ failures driven by systemic inflammation

subtypes.name: Hepatic Encephalopathy in Cirrhosis
subtypes.pathophysiology: Portosystemic shunting of gut-derived neurotoxins (ammonia, manganese, benzodiazepine-like compounds) bypassing hepatic metabolism, causing astrocyte swelling and cerebral dysfunction
examples: precipitated HE (bleeding, infection, TIPS, constipation, electrolyte imbalance)

subtypes.name: Hyperacute ALF
subtypes.pathophysiology: Jaundice-to-encephalopathy interval < 7 days, massive necrosis, high risk cerebral edema but paradoxically better recovery potential

mimics: Fulminant Wilson disease (acute hemolytic crisis, very low alkaline phosphatase, Kayser-Fleischer rings)
Reye syndrome: acute encephalopathy + fatty liver in children with aspirin use during viral illness
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy: third trimester, preeclampsia features, liver dysfunction, DIC
Sepsis-associated encephalopathy: altered mental status in septic patient without liver failure
Wernicke encephalopathy: oculomotor signs, ataxia, confusion, thiamine deficiency in chronic liver disease

investigations.detail: Send for ALL cases of ALF regardless of history. Must check even if patient denies ingestion. Levels > 10 ug/mL with elevated transaminases > 1000 IU/L suggests APAP toxicity.
Anti-HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, anti-HEV IgM, HSV PCR, EBV serology, CMV PCR, VZV PCR. Hepatitis E is underdiagnosed cause of ALF in US.
Low ceruloplasmin < 20 mg/dL + Kayser-Fleischer rings confirms Wilson disease. Also check: total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase (AP). AP-to-bilirubin ratio < 4 suggests Wilsonian ALF.

Arterial ammonia preferred over venous (venous 1-2x higher). No correlation between absolute ammonia level and HE severity in cirrhosis. In ALF, ammonia > 150-200 ug/dL associated with cerebral edema. False elevation: blood drawn with tourniquet, stored on ice, in smokers.

CT head: exclude ICH, cerebral edema (effacement of sulci, loss of grey-white differentiation), herniation. ICP monitoring: controversial, considered in ALF grade 3-4 encephalopathy at transplant centers. Jugular venous oximetry for cerebral O2 extraction ratio.

Considered in selected ALF cases where etiology uncertain and autoimmune or malignancy suspected. Risks: bleeding (coagulopathy). Tissue can show massive necrosis, microvesicular steatosis, or specific viral inclusions.

investigations.test: TIPS Evaluation: Hepatic Venous Pressure Gradient (HVPG)
investigations.detail: Measured via transjugular approach. Portal hypertension defined as HVPG > 5 mmHg; clinically significant > 10 mmHg. TIPS indicated for refractory ascites, recurrent variceal bleeding, and selected cases of hydrothorax. Contraindications: severe hepatic encephalopathy, severe liver failure (MELD > 18), right heart failure, pulmonary hypertension.

pathophysiology.summary: Systemic accumulation of gut-derived neurotoxins (primarily ammonia) due to hepatic failure and portosystemic shunting. Ammonia enters the brain, is metabolized by astrocytes to glutamine via glutamine synthetase, causing osmotic astrocyte swelling, cerebral edema, and neuronal dysfunction. Neuroinflammation, manganese deposition, and altered GABA/benzodiazepine neurotransmission contribute.
mechanisms: Ammonia: from gut urease-producing bacteria, normally cleared by liver; directs conversion to glutamine in astrocytes leading to osmotic edema
Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) synergistically worsens CNS dysfunction by disrupting blood-brain barrier
In ALF, rapid rise of ICP from cerebral edema; herniation is the leading cause of death
Altered neurotransmitter systems: increased GABA-ergic tone (endogenous benzodiazepines), decreased glutamate uptake, serotonergic dysfunction

complications: Cerebral edema and intracranial hypertension (leading cause of death in ALF)
Herniation syndromes: uncal, central transtentorial, cerebellar tonsillar
Multiorgan failure: renal failure (hepatorenal syndrome), respiratory failure, cardiovascular collapse, coagulopathy
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, aspiration pneumonia, catheter-related bloodstream infection
Coagulopathy from impaired clotting factor synthesis (INR > 6.5) despite elevated D-dimer and low antithrombin III

management.protocol: ICP Monitoring and Intracranial Hypertension Management
management.detail: Consider ICP monitor (subdural or intraparenchymal) in ALF with grade 3-4 encephalopathy awaiting transplant at experienced centers. Goals: ICP < 20-25 mmHg, CPP > 50-60 mmHg. First-line: head elevation 30 degrees, avoid hyperthermia/ hypoxia/hypotension. Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV bolus for acute ICP spikes. Hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg) for herniation emergencies only (brief, as it reduces CBF). Second-line: pentobarbital coma, moderate hypothermia (33-35 C). Third-line: decompressive hemicraniectomy. Per AASLD guidelines.

management.protocol: Lactulose and Rifaximin for Hepatic Encephalopathy
management.detail: Lactulose: initial 30 mL PO/NG q1h until 2-3 loose stools, then titrate to 2-3 bowel movements/day. Mechanism: acidifies colon, converts NH3 to non-absorbable NH4+, reduces ammonia-producing bacteria. Rifaximin: 550 mg PO/NG BID as adjunct to lactulose for HE reversal and secondary prophylaxis. Combined therapy superior to either alone (mechanism: non-absorbable antibiotic reduces gut urease-producing bacteria).

management.protocol: N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) for Acetaminophen and Non-Acetaminophen ALF
management.detail: Acetaminophen ALF: 150 mg/kg in D5W over 1 hour, then 12.5 mg/kg/hr for 4 hours, then 6.25 mg/kg/hr continued until INR < 2. Duration typically 20-72 hours. Most effective within 8-12 hours of ingestion. Non-acetaminophen ALF: 150 mg/kg/day for 72 hours (benefit shown in pediatric ALF not in adult RCT).

Criteria for urgent listing: King's College Criteria, MELD score > 40 on waitlist. Absolute contraindications: fixed cerebral herniation, uncontrolled extra-cerebral infection, metastatic hepatic malignancy. Relative contraindications: age > 70, severe psychiatric illness, active substance abuse, poor social support. One-year survival after transplant for ALF: 85-90%.

Consider in chronic HE with recurrent ascites/variceal bleeding, NOT during acute HE episode (TIPS worsens HE). Pre-TIPS evaluation: HVPG measurement, hepatic and portal vein patency, liver biopsy if needed, cardiac evaluation (right heart catheterization to exclude pulmonary hypertension). Contraindications: severe HE (West Haven grade 2+), MELD > 18, severe pulmonary hypertension, right heart failure, polycystic liver disease.

Do NOT correct INR with FFP unless active bleeding or invasive procedure. INR is a marker of severity, not a bleeding risk (ALF has rebalanced hemostasis despite elevated INR). For invasive procedures: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelet transfusion, tranexamic acid. Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily for 3 days to exclude deficiency. Per SCCM guidelines for hemostatic management in liver disease.

Enteral nutrition preferred within 24-48 hours. Protein restriction is NOT recommended in hepatic encephalopathy. Protein: 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day (cirrhosis) to 1.5-2.0 g/kg/day (ALF with stress). Branched-chain amino acids may be beneficial in HE. Thiamine 100 mg IV before dextrose-containing fluids. Avoid hepatotoxic medications.
